text.title: python-web-frameworks Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for python-web-frameworks as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Mixing framework idioms: @app.route in FastAPI, User.objects in FastAPI routes, Flask-Login in FastAPI projects.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Each framework has its own routing, ORM, and auth primitives. Mixing causes silent failures (Flask imports in FastAPI are resolved but behave differently) or runtime errors (User.objects requires django.setup()).

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Deprecated in FastAPI 0.93+. Use the lifespan context manager instead.

failure-mode.description: Quoting the unsourced RPS benchmark numbers (1,500/3,000+ RPS) as authoritative.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: These numbers are context-dependent and not from a named benchmark. Always run a project-specific load test before citing performance claims.

failure-mode.description: Starting with Flask for "simplicity" then adding Flask-RESTX, Flask-Migrate, Flask-Login, Flask-WTF until the dep list mirrors Django.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: At that point Flask adds friction without adding features. The decision matrix should have chosen Django upfront.

failure-mode.description: Using Flask async views expecting real concurrency under WSGI.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: WSGI is synchronous. Async views run in a sync wrapper — no concurrency benefit, just added complexity.
